summary: An already-authenticated visitor who navigates to /login is shown the empty sign-in form instead of being redirected to /dashboard.
reproduction: Log in successfully (e.g. as admin/admin123) so a valid JWT is stored under localStorage['aether_token'].
With that session still valid, navigate the browser directly to https://5cb5f0620.abacusai.cloud/login.
Observe the page: the Sign in form renders with empty fields; there is no redirect to /dashboard.
expected: A visitor who already holds a valid session and lands on /login should be forwarded to /dashboard (or at minimum shown some acknowledgement they are already signed in) rather than being re-presented an empty login form, which is the common pattern for auth screens and avoids user confusion about session state.
observed: apps/web/src/app/login/page.tsx performs no localStorage/session check on mount; the page always renders the sign-in form regardless of an existing valid token. Reproduced with both a synthetic fake token and a real admin JWT obtained from the one permitted admin login.

summary: Unauthenticated deep-link to a dashboard sub-route redirects to bare /login without preserving the originally-requested destination; the login flow always sends the user to plain /dashboard afterward, never back to the page they tried to reach.
reproduction: In a fresh, unauthenticated browser session, navigate directly to https://5cb5f0620.abacusai.cloud/dashboard/resume-studio.
Observe: AuthGuard redirects to https://5cb5f0620.abacusai.cloud/login with no query parameter (no ?next=, ?redirect=, or similar) referencing the original /dashboard/resume-studio destination.
Code inspection confirms apps/web/src/app/login/page.tsx:40 always calls router.push('/dashboard') on successful login, with no mechanism to read or honor an intended-destination parameter.
expected: A common, user-friendly pattern is to return the user to the page they originally tried to reach after they sign in (e.g. /login?next=/dashboard/resume-studio -> redirect to /dashboard/resume-studio post-login). This is explicitly called out as a testable behavior in the tester brief.
observed: The intended destination is silently dropped; every successful login lands on the generic /dashboard home regardless of what the user was trying to reach.

summary: No logout/sign-out affordance exists anywhere in the reachable UI (topbar, sidebar, dashboard settings, admin settings) — a signed-in session can only end by the 24h JWT expiring or by manually clearing browser storage.
reproduction: Log in (admin/admin123, the one permitted login for this session) and inspect the dashboard shell (Topbar, Sidebar) and Settings page for any Sign out / Log out control.

Source-code grep confirms: no component calls clearToken() or removes the aether_token localStorage key in response to a user action anywhere in apps/web/src (clearToken() is only invoked automatically by the API client on a 401 response, never by a user-facing button).
expected: A production auth screen's session lifecycle should include a user-initiated way to end the session (sign out), especially since the app is used on potentially shared/public devices and holds a 24h-lived bearer token in localStorage.
observed: No sign-out control exists in the codebase. This was discovered while testing session persistence and the login->dashboard journey per the brief's instruction to 'log out if a logout affordance exists' -- none exists to test.
evidence: grep -rn logout|clearToken apps/web/src -> only apps/web/src/lib/api/client.ts:59 (automatic 401 handler, not user-facing)

summary: No 'Forgot password' / password-reset flow exists on the login screen or anywhere in the application (frontend or backend).
reproduction: Load /login and inspect all links/text near the password field and footer: only 'Create account' is present, no 'Forgot password?' link.
grep -rn for forgot/reset password across apps/web/src and apps/api/app: zero matches.
expected: A standard production login screen offers a self-service password-recovery path for a user who forgets their credentials.
observed: No such affordance exists on the UI, and no backend endpoint implements it. A user locked out of their account (e.g., forgotten password) has no in-product recovery path.
evidence: test-artifacts/01-initial-load.png (full-page screenshot shows no forgot-password link)
